exemplar.title: How can Cinnabon enhance its market penetration strategy by launching a new branch in City Mall in 2025?

detailedExemplarData.title: How can Cinnabon enhance its market penetration strategy by launching a new branch in City Mall in 2025?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Demonstrates a clear grasp of the key concept of change, framing it consistently as a structural and strategic shift.
Effectively embeds the concept within analytical tools (e.g., Force-Field and break-even analyses).
Maintains a strong connection to Cinnabon Azerbaijan throughout the IA.
weaknesses: The concept is not used as the primary analytical lens in every section (e.g., some SWOT points drift into generic market entry discussion).
Quantitative inputs from the appendix are accepted without critical validation, weakening full integration.
The conclusion does not explicitly tie every recommendation back to the dynamics of change.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student displays strong understanding of âchangeâ with partial integration throughout most sections, matching Band 4. To reach Band 5 they would need seamless, primary-lens usage of the concept in every analytical discussion.

strengths: Includes a Force-Field graphic, break-even chart, survey summary and interview transcripts, satisfying the range requirement.
Visual break-even chart effectively reinforces the operational feasibility analysis.
weaknesses: Supporting documents lack depth: small survey sample, summarised financial table without full itemisation.
Force-Field figure is unlabeled and misformatted, hindering interpretation of force magnitudes.
Overreliance on informal online sources reduces the evidentiary strength.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Documents are relevant but their limited depth, formatting issues and source weaknesses place this at Band 2 rather than the satisfactory Band 3 level.

strengths: Selection of SWOT, Force-Field and break-even tools is appropriate for a market-entry decision.
Each tool is applied directly to the research question, linking factors to customer demand and cost structures.
Methodology outlines a coherent mixed-method approach, connecting primary/secondary data to analysis.
weaknesses: Tool set is limitedâomits complementary analyses (e.g., PEST, ratio analysis) that could enrich insights.
Minor calculation inconsistencies (currency symbols, unexplained fixed-cost inputs) are present.
Force-Field weights lack clear justification and detailed cost classification procedures are omitted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows adequate selection and clear application of tools (Band 3). Broader tool coverage and tighter application would be needed for higher marks.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Integrates survey and interview data within analytical tools to support findings.
Connects break-even figures to daily sales capacity, linking data to operational implications.
weaknesses: Limited statistical robustness due to a 25-respondent survey and lack of discussion on sampling bias.
Analysis often remains brief, lacking multi-angle evaluation (e.g., no sensitivity testing).
Numerical justifications (e.g., Force-Field weights, average selling price formula) are under-explained, reducing transparency.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While analysis goes beyond description and uses data adequately at times, its narrow scope, brevity and lack of depth align more closely with Band 2 than a fully integrated Band 3 evaluation.

strengths: Conclusions restate key numerical findings and balance opportunities against risks, fully consistent with the analysis.
Explicitly answers the research question with clear, direct recommendations on branch opening, cost control and promotions.
weaknesses: Recommendations could be structured by short-, medium- and long-term actions for more practical guidance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is fully consistent with evidence and directly answers the RQ, meeting all Band 3 requirements.

strengths: Logical, sustained organization with matching headings and a comprehensive table of contents.
Clear sectional divisions (methodology, analysis, conclusion, appendices) facilitate reader navigation.
weaknesses: Could benefit from nested sub-headings (e.g., within SWOT) to improve micro-structure visibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The IA demonstrates appropriate structure throughout, fulfilling Band 2 criteria for clear organization.

strengths: All required IB elements are present: title page, RQ, word count, methodology, analysis, conclusion, references, appendices.
Title page clearly outlines RQ and key concept, showing compliance with presentation standards.
weaknesses: Inconsistent reference formatting (author, date, title, URL) across entries.
Some source captions are vague or incomplete, reducing transparency of evidence origins.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite minor referencing inconsistencies, the submission includes all mandatory elements and therefore earns full marks for presentation (Band 2).

strandSelections.reasoning: The IA follows a logical structure with title page, contents, methodology, analysis, conclusion and appendices.  Headings match table-of-contents entries, facilitating navigation.  This represents appropriate, sustained organisationâBand 2 (maximum available).

All supporting documents directly inform the investigationâfinancial figures feed the break-even, the survey informs demand, and interviews reveal managerial intentions.  Depth, however, is modest: the survey is only 25 respondents and the financial table is summarised rather than fully itemised, limiting the richness of insight.  Relevance is good but depth only âsufficientâ, matching Band 3.

All required IB elementsâtitle page with RQ, word count, methodology, analysis, conclusion, reference list and appendicesâare present.  Hence the submission satisfies the full presentation criterion, warranting the top score of 2.

The submission includes a Force-Field graphic, a break-even chart, the survey summary and interview transcripts (mentioned in text), satisfying the 3-5 document range.  The quantity criterion is therefore met at the standard Band 3 level.

Each tool is applied to the research question: the SWOT links strengths to attracting mall visitors, the Force-Field quantifies drivers and restraints, and the break-even provides concrete sales targets.  Nonetheless, calculations show minor inconsistencies (currency symbols, unexplained fixed-cost figure) and the Force-Field weights lack justification, so relevance is clear but application only âadequateâ.  That aligns with Band 3.

Data are drawn from a small survey, two interviews and headline financialsâenough to support analysis but limited in scope and statistical robustness.  The student uses these data within each tool and integrates them into commentary, showing adequacy rather than merely superficial use.  Consequently, Band 3 is the best fit.

Analysis goes beyond description: threats in the SWOT are interpreted in light of survey spending limits, and the break-even figure is connected to daily sales capacity.  Still, discussion often remains brief and lacks multi-angle evaluation (e.g., no sensitivity test on break-even).  Integration is present but not sustained or thorough, so Band 3 is appropriate.

The research questionââHow can Cinnabon enhance its market penetration by launching a new branch in City Mall?ââis explicitly answered with the recommendation to open, conditional on cost control and tailored promotions.  The answer is direct and unambiguous, fitting Band 3.

The student selects three mainstream toolsâSWOT, Force-Field and Break-Evenâappropriate to a market-entry decision.  While adequate, the tool set is limited; no ratio analysis, BCG, Ansoff or PEST is attempted.  This represents an âadequate selectionâ rather than a particularly broad or strategic mix, fitting Band 3.

The conclusion restates key numerical findings (foot traffic, break-even in 8.2 months) and balances them against risks (economic downturn, competition), mirroring evidence presented.  Therefore, it is fully consistent with the analysis, meriting the top band for this strand.

The assessment is tightly focused on Cinnabon Azerbaijan and its proposed branch in City Mall.  Primary data (interviews with the country manager and an employee, a customer survey) and cost figures from the franchisor are embedded in each tool, and the conclusion explicitly weighs City Mall foot-traffic and local spending patterns.  The organisation is therefore analysed consistently rather than merely described.  Nevertheless, some quantitative inputs (e.g., exact fixed costs, margin calculations) are taken from the appendix without critical validation, and several claims about brand awareness remain anecdotal, preventing âfullâ integration.  Thus the evidence matches a strong, but not comprehensive, organisational connectionâBand 4.

The student demonstrates a clear grasp of the key concept of change, repeatedly framing the expansion as a âstructural and strategic changeâ and linking the need for adjustments in operations, marketing and technology to maintain competitiveness.  References to change are carried forward into each analytical tool (e.g., âfinancial and operational change will be neededâ in the Force-Field and âthis expansion requires both a structural and strategic changeâ in the break-even section).  However, while the concept is regularly mentioned, it is not used as the primary analytical lens throughout every section; parts of the SWOT and recommendations drift toward generic market entry issues without tying them explicitly back to the dynamics of change.  This reflects strong understanding with partial, but not fully seamless, integrationâbest aligned with Band 4.

annotations.comment: The breadth of supporting documents demonstrates thorough research, providing multiple data types.
The table of contents is comprehensive and aligns with IA requirements, aiding clear navigation through the report.
The methodology section effectively outlines the mixed-method approach and links primary and secondary data to the RQ.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Expand on how interview protocols were designed to explore aspects of organizational change to bolster methodological transparency.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: A survey sample of 25 limits statistical robustness; discuss sampling bias or consider a larger sample for more reliable demand insights.
The SWOT table is well organized, presenting key factors clearly across strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Strengths and weaknesses rely on narrative; incorporating quantitative metrics (e.g., brand-awareness percentages) would deepen analytical rigor.
The partial Force Field table shows misformatted entries (âAZN on food.10â); ensure the full table and figures render correctly.
Provide justification for the numerical weights in the Force Field analysis to improve transparency and analytical credibility.
The title page clearly presents the RQ and key concept alongside the required IB elements, demonstrating strong compliance with presentation standards.
The research question is explicit, but the introduction could more directly frame how âchangeâ will be analyzed throughout the IA to sharpen the analytical lens.
Consider adding nested sub-headings for sections like SWOT sub-analyses in the TOC to improve structural clarity.
The introduction gives solid company and market context, providing a clear foundation for subsequent analysis.
While market shifts are described, the text stops short of analytically linking these to the key concept of change; explicitly tie demographic changes to strategic change.
The main results integrate survey and interview data to profile demand and awareness, supporting subsequent analysis.
The equation for average selling price appears as $$(10+12.1)/2 = 11.05$$ without clear context of components; specify unit prices and sources.
The contribution margin calculation is correct, but explicitly stating $$	ext{CM} = SP - VC$$ will prevent reader confusion.
Clarify the days-to-break-even calculation: dividing 28,571 units by 114 units/day yields ~250 days (â8.3 months); specify average month days.
The break-even chart visually reinforces the analysis, helping readers see the operational timeline and feasibility.
The conclusion synthesizes the main findings well and acknowledges both feasibility and key risks.
To fully integrate the key concept, the conclusion should explicitly reflect how strategic change underpins each recommendation.
The limitations section rightly flags sample size and volatility, but should propose concrete measures (e.g., follow-up surveys) to address these.
Organizing recommendations by short-, medium-, and long-term actions could enhance practical implementation planning.
Reference entries vary in formatting style; standardize citation elements (author, date, title, URL) for consistency.
Some online sources lack access dates or full URLs (e.g., MarketLine report); ensure each reference is complete and up to date.
The description of the Force Field concept effectively links to the strategic change framework, showing theoretical integration.
The Force Field figure lacks labeled values on axes, making it hard to gauge force magnitudes; add clear labeling.
Include an explicit formula for total fixed costs here to clarify how the break-even point inputs were derived.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The source caption â(julius.ai, n.d.)â is vague; provide full reference or describe the data origin for transparency.
Reliance on informal online sources (blogs) may weaken evidence strength; supplement with peer-reviewed or industry data where possible.
Could the research question define specific performance metrics (e.g., target penetration rate) to sharpen analytical focus?
The methodology refers to cost determinants but omits detail on how fixed versus variable costs were classified; include a clear cost-classification process.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: When presenting interview findings, direct quotations can bolster authenticity and illustrate stakeholder perspectives.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Consider adding a quantitative scoring layer to the SWOT matrix (e.g., weighted scores) to prioritize key factors and enrich evaluation.
The Force Field description correctly embeds the change framework, demonstrating clear integration of concept theory.
